scenario: Orion Healthcare runs Camunda 8.8 across 7 Spring Boot services with 28 job workers handling 16000 patient-record events per hour. The team has set fetch-variables under both camunda.client.worker.defaults and camunda.client.worker.override.<job-type> for a specific handler and must reconcile how the two lists combine.
question: How does the 8.8 reference describe fetch-variables under camunda.client.worker.override compared to defaults?

options.text: The 8.8 reference documents that fetch-variables under override replaces the list of variables to fetch while under defaults it extends the annotation list.
The 8.8 reference documents that fetch-variables under override extends the defaults list while under defaults it extends the annotation list of variables.
The 8.8 reference documents that fetch-variables under override is ignored while under defaults it replaces the annotation list of variables to fetch.
The 8.8 reference documents that fetch-variables under override and under defaults both replace the annotation list of variables to fetch in 8.8 workers.

a.text: Correct. The 8.8 reference says fetch-variables under defaults extends the list of variables from the annotation, while under override it replaces the list.

b.text: Incorrect. Override entries do not extend defaults; the reference documents replacement semantics for the override block specifically.

c.text: Incorrect. Defaults extend, not replace; and override entries are honored, not ignored, when the job type matches the keyed entry.

d.text: Incorrect. Defaults extend the annotation list; only override entries replace the list, so the two scopes behave differently.

explanation: fetch-variables under camunda.client.worker.defaults extends the annotation list of variables to fetch. The same property under camunda.client.worker.override.<job-type> replaces that list for the keyed job type.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference
